nativeRange.habitat: Shallow to subtidal reef zones on rocky and rubble substrate; photic zone

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product photo from Mosaic Macros listing — sole hobby source for this species

summary: Tubular Thicket (Galaxaura rugosa) is a rare lightly calcified red macroalgae with a reddish-pink, tubular-branching thallus that grows into a compact spherical form. It demands stable calcium, alkalinity, and high lighting, and is generally considered an advanced-care species available only through specialty retailers.
careNotes:
Tubular Thicket is a lightly calcified red macroalgae in the genus Galaxaura (family Galaxauraceae, order Nemaliales, class Florideophyceae, division Rhodophyta). The thallus — the plant body of an alga — is reddish-pink, grows in a compact spherical form, and has a distinctly tubular branching structure. Cuttings are typically sold on a plug at 2–3" or as larger 5"+ portions.

Because G. rugosa deposits calcium carbonate into its thallus, stable calcium and magnesium levels are generally considered important for long-term health. Calcium is broadly advised around 400–450 ppm and magnesium around 1250–1350 ppm. Standard reef water parameters apply otherwise: specific gravity 1.023–1.026, temperature 22–26°C (72–78°F), pH 8.1–8.4.

Lighting: High light output is generally beneficial for this species. Full-spectrum LED is recommended; actinic-only spectrum (blue-heavy lighting without a significant white component) tends to be insufficient for more demanding macroalgae in this family.

Flow: Flow should be gentle — roughly comparable to a soft-coral display tank. Excessive flow has been reported to damage delicate branching forms.

Placement: Tubular Thicket must not be buried in sand or substrate. Red algae that contact sediment at the base tend to melt from the point of contact upward. Attachment to live rock is strongly preferred, either by wedging the base into a crevice or securing it with fishing line or a zip-tie until it attaches naturally; wedging is generally considered the least stressful method.

Growth rate is generally slow, consistent with the metabolic cost of skeletal calcification. Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and other trace elements is considered beneficial for sustained growth. Propagation is by fragmentation of the branching thallus.

This is a rare species available only through a small number of specialty retailers, and stock is often limited. Care parameters are drawn from genus- and family-level knowledge of Galaxauraceae alongside general macroalgae husbandry guidance; individual results may vary. Related display specimens in the same family include Galaxaura sp. ('Bramble Thicket', [sw-algae-020]), Galaxaura oblongata ('Pink Succulent Thicket', [sw-algae-074]), and Dichotomaria marginata ('Flat-Branched Pink Thicket', [sw-algae-041]), all sharing calcified morphology and similar reef husbandry requirements.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source. Confirmed scientific name Galaxaura rugosa, trade name, morphology (tubular-branching, reddish-pink, spherical growth form, lightly calcified), and cutting size tiers (2–3" plug, 5"+). Minimal explicit husbandry parameters on product page.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Macroalgae Care Guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source. Provided authoritative water parameter targets (temp 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm, NO3 5–20 ppm, PO4 0.02–0.1 ppm), lighting guidance (full-spectrum LED; no actinic-only), flow guidance, rock-attachment requirement for red algae (no sand-bed burial), propagation methods (wedging, zip-tie/fishing line, fragmentation), and micronutrient dosing recommendations.
